synopsis: Moises Naim argues that power across governments, corporations, militaries, and religions has become easier to acquire but harder to exercise and sustain. Drawing on global examples, he shows how micropowers now challenge once-dominant megaplayers, forcing a rethinking of how authority functions in the modern world.

sources.title: The End of Power - Hachette Book Group

synopsis: Peter Zeihan maps the consequences of deglobalization, arguing that the American-led global order established after World War II is unraveling, leaving countries to secure their own food, energy, and goods with aging, shrinking populations. The book traces how different regions and industries will fare as global supply chains fragment and international trade collapses.

sources.title: The End of the World Is Just the Beginning - Wikipedia

synopsis: Dave Weisberg chronicles the history of computer-aided design from its research roots at MIT through the development of major commercial CAD systems and companies, drawing on his experience as a participant in the field's early decades. The book traces how CAD technologies transformed engineering practice, changing not just the speed and accuracy of design work but the entire approach engineers take to their craft.

sources.title: The Engineering Design Revolution CAD History (full text)

synopsis: This collection gathers over fifty essays by contributors to the LessWrong rationalist community, covering topics from epistemology and incentives to modularity in complex systems and civilizational risk. The essays take varied forms—thought experiments, literature reviews, philosophical arguments, and mathematical explanations—unified by an effort to understand and improve the art of rationality.

sources.title: Book Launch: The Engines of Cognition - LessWrong

synopsis: Darren Hardy prepares prospective entrepreneurs for the emotional volatility of starting and running a business, using a roller coaster metaphor to describe the inevitable highs and lows they will face. The book focuses on four skills Hardy identifies as critical to entrepreneurial success: sales, recruiting, leadership, and productivity.

sources.title: The Entrepreneur Roller Coaster - Darren Hardy Store

synopsis: This anthology compiles twenty-six selections from Drucker's major management works, organized around three themes—management, the individual, and society—drawn from six decades of his writing. It presents core frameworks for executives and managers dealing with the challenges of organizational life, including what nonprofits can teach business, how information executives need has changed, and how to navigate the second half of a career.

A curated selection from the original Whole Earth Catalog, this volume presents tools, books, products, and ideas spanning ecology, land use, architecture, health, crafts, education, and communication. It functions as a reference and gateway, connecting readers to practical resources across a broad range of domains oriented toward self-sufficiency and alternative approaches to modern life.

sources.title: The Essential Whole Earth Catalog - Amazon

synopsis: Hobbit Frodo Baggins inherits the One Ring forged by the dark lord Sauron to dominate Middle-earth, and sets out with a diverse company of humans, elves, dwarves, and hobbits to destroy it in the fires of Mount Doom. The journey leads them from the Shire through perilous mountains and underground mines before the fellowship is broken at the edge of Mordor.

sources.title: The Fellowship of the Ring - Britannica

synopsis: The Feynman Lectures on Physics is a three-volume textbook drawn from Richard Feynman's undergraduate courses at Caltech in the early 1960s, covering mechanics, radiation, heat, electromagnetism, and quantum mechanics. Feynman uses clear language and everyday examples to build physical intuition, making the lectures a foundational resource for students and physicists seeking deep conceptual understanding.

sources.title: The Feynman Lectures on Physics - Hachette Book Group

synopsis: Peter Senge argues that the only sustainable competitive advantage for any organization is the ability to learn faster than competitors, and he presents five disciplines—systems thinking, personal mastery, mental models, shared vision, and team learning—that together define a learning organization. The book draws on case studies from major companies to show how these disciplines can be developed and integrated in practice.

sources.title: The Fifth Discipline - Penguin Random House

synopsis: Michael Lewis examines the federal government's vast and underappreciated risk management functions by profiling career officials at the Departments of Energy, Agriculture, and Commerce who were displaced or sidelined during the 2016 presidential transition. The book argues that the incoming administration's indifference to these agencies created serious vulnerabilities in functions ranging from food safety and weather prediction to nuclear security.

On a world called the Stillness where catastrophic seismic events periodically end civilizations, Essun must hide her orogene powers—the ability to manipulate seismic energy—while searching for her daughter after her husband kills their son and disappears. Three converging narratives examine how this society survives repeated apocalypses by systematically oppressing the very people capable of controlling geological catastrophe.

Nathaniel Moon, born with complete consciousness and miraculous abilities, has spent his life performing anonymous acts of healing as a hospital janitor in Middle Falls, Oregon, deliberately avoiding the attention his powers would attract. When a violent threat to his goddaughter forces him to act openly, his carefully maintained obscurity unravels, changing his life and those around him.

sources.title: The Final Life of Nathaniel Moon - Amazon

synopsis: Harry August dies and is reborn into the same life repeatedly, retaining all memories, and belongs to a hidden network of similarly looping individuals called the Cronus Club. When a dying child delivers a warning that the end of the world is accelerating with each cycle, Harry must identify who is deliberately hastening history toward catastrophe.

sources.title: The First Fifteen Lives of Harry August - Hachette Book Group

synopsis: Using a business fable structured around a fictional CEO named Kathryn Petersen who must rebuild a failing executive team, Patrick Lencioni identifies five behavioral dysfunctions that undermine team performance: absence of trust, fear of conflict, lack of commitment, avoidance of accountability, and inattention to results. The second half of the book presents a practical model for diagnosing and overcoming each dysfunction.

sources.title: The Five Dysfunctions of a Team - Porchlight Books

synopsis: This condensed summary of Patrick Lencioni's Five Dysfunctions of a Team distills the original book's core framework for readers who want the key concepts without the full business fable narrative. It covers the five dysfunctions—absence of trust, fear of conflict, lack of commitment, avoidance of accountability, and inattention to results—along with practical guidance for addressing each one.

sources.title: The Five Dysfunctions of a Team Summarized for Busy People - Goodreads

synopsis: Private William Mandella is drafted into an interstellar war against the alien Taurans, and relativistic time dilation means each tour of duty returns him to an Earth centuries older and unrecognizable. The novel uses this premise to examine the absurdity of war and the alienation of soldiers who outlive the civilization they fought to protect.

Jimmy Soni reconstructs PayPal's turbulent founding years through hundreds of interviews and access to internal documents, tracing the clashes between Confinity and X.com that eventually merged into the company and exploring the outsized ambitions of figures like Peter Thiel and Elon Musk. The book examines how the network that emerged from PayPal's early battles went on to reshape Silicon Valley's culture and investment landscape.

Rob Copeland investigates Bridgewater Associates and its founder Ray Dalio through hundreds of interviews with current and former employees, challenging the public image Dalio built around his published principles. The book portrays a firm where surveillance, internal conflict, and a culture of radical transparency as practiced differed sharply from the management philosophy Dalio promoted externally.
